title: Login redirect loop for users with plus-addressed or aliased emails after Okta → Entra ID migration
problem: After migrating SSO from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID, approximately 30% of users experience an infinite redirect loop during login. The user authenticates successfully with Entra ID but TaskFlow fails to establish a session, sending them back to the IdP repeatedly. The affected population correlates strongly with users whose email addresses contain a '+' character (plus addressing) or whose email addresses changed/were aliased during the Entra ID setup.
root_cause_hypothesis: TaskFlow's SSO callback handler matches the incoming identity token's email claim against stored user records to look up or provision the local session. Entra ID likely returns the email claim in a different format than Okta did — either URL-encoding the '+' as '%2B', stripping the plus-address tag entirely, normalizing casing differently, or returning a different alias than the one stored in TaskFlow's user table. When the lookup fails, TaskFlow treats the user as unauthenticated and redirects back to the IdP, creating the loop. The partial success of clearing cookies suggests that stale Okta OIDC state cookies (nonce, session, or CSRF tokens) may also interfere with the new auth flow on some attempts.
reproduction_steps: Create or identify a TaskFlow user account whose email contains a '+' character (e.g. jane+taskflow@company.com)
Attempt to log in via the Microsoft Entra ID SSO flow
Observe that after successful Entra ID authentication, the browser is redirected back to TaskFlow and then immediately redirected to Entra ID again in a loop
Compare the email claim value in the Entra ID token (decode the id_token JWT) against the email stored in TaskFlow's user database for that user
environment: TaskFlow instance recently migrated from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID SSO. Affects users with plus-addressed emails or emails that changed during migration. Browser-independent (occurs across browsers, partially mitigated by clearing cookies or incognito).
impact: ~30% of the team is locked out of TaskFlow entirely. Workarounds (clearing cookies, incognito) are unreliable. This blocks daily work for affected users.
recommended_fix: 1. **Inspect the token claims:** Log or decode the id_token returned by Entra ID for an affected user and compare the email/preferred_username/UPN claim against what TaskFlow has stored. Identify the exact mismatch (encoding, alias, normalization). 2. **Fix user lookup:** Normalize email comparison in the SSO callback — at minimum, URL-decode the claim value and do case-insensitive matching. Consider matching on a stable claim like `oid` (Entra object ID) or `sub` rather than email. 3. **Backfill user records:** For users whose emails changed during migration, either update TaskFlow's stored emails to match what Entra ID returns, or add a mapping/alias table. 4. **Clear stale auth cookies:** On the SSO callback endpoint, clear any legacy Okta session cookies (e.g. old nonce or CSRF cookies) that may interfere with the new flow.
proposed_test_case: Write an integration test that simulates an SSO callback with an id_token containing a plus-addressed email (e.g. 'jane+taskflow@company.com') and verifies that TaskFlow correctly matches it to the existing user record and establishes a session without redirecting back to the IdP. Add a second case where the token email is a known alias that differs from the stored email to verify alias resolution.
information_gaps: Exact claim name Entra ID is returning the email in (email vs preferred_username vs UPN) — needs a token decode to confirm
Whether TaskFlow uses a library for OIDC or has custom callback handling — affects where the fix goes
Whether any legacy Okta OIDC cookie names conflict with the new Entra ID flow
